EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked from the Gulf of Alaska to the Desert Southwest, bringing high winds to the Mojave Desert.
Strong winds resulted as a low pressure system and cold front raced across the Southwestern states.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of blowing dust resulted throughout many desert locations during mid day. One of the strongest peak wind gusts was recorded at MCAS Yuma where 38 knot winds hit that airport at 12:22 PM MST. The visibility was reduced to near 1 mile.  A spotter in Yuma reported 1/4 mile visibility briefly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Visibility was reduced to less than 1/4 mile near Globe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility reported to be as low as 100 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowstorm caused multiple traffic accidents along Interstate 40 between Flagstaff and Williams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Pacific winter storm created hazardous driving conditions on I-40 between Flagstaff and Williams.  The freeway was closed while crews cleared a semi trailer that had rolled over.  Two other semis rolled over in the same area. There were a total of 10 collisions and 12 cars that slid off the road. Travel was also difficult on I-17, Highway 89, and Highway 180 around Flagstaff. There were 5 non-injury accidents and two injury accidents within the Flagstaff City limits.  There were 6 non-injury and 1 injury accidents reported by Coconino County law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low moved just south of Arizona November 29th into 30th. This resulted in significant snowfall in the higher elevations of Graham and Cochise Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 20 inches of snow occurred at the top of Mt. Graham at an elevation above 10,000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped heavy rain over portions of Sedona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over Sedona and flooded a portion of Highway 179 blocking traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain around Cottonwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three feet of flowing water in a low water crossing trapped one car.  A swift water rescue was performed on the driver.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee and members of the general public reported possible funnel cloud over Flagstaff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning bolt hit the ground near three people that had stopped riding their motorcycles because of the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four motorcyclist stopped on the shoulder of a roadway because of the heavy rain.  One of them hid in a near by culvert.  While stopped,  lightning hit the ground near the three cyclists knocking them on the ground.  All three were air lifted to hospitals.  The woman had badly burned feet.  One man was very disoriented.  The third man was unconscious.  The current had entered his feet and exited his head in three places.  He died two days later in the hospital.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alamo Lake State Park reported damage to several RV's due to winds. APS power outage lasted for about 9 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain of Cochise County...including the Pinaleno Mountains. A dry boundary layer during this pre-monsoon environment encouraged the development of strong thunderstorm outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm outflow wind gust estimated at 60 mph destroyed a horse barn on the Diamond P Ranch on Brookerson Road, 17 miles north of Willcox. The 10-stall horse barn was completely destroyed by the wind gust. Two racehorses that were not in the barn were killed, when debris from the barn landed on them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With high pressure aloft over the Four-Corners region...northeast flow aloft prevailed over Southeast Arizona. Thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain northeast of Tucson...and propogated southwestward. With the boundary layer still relatively dry...thunderstorms were able to aggregate and strengthen their outflow winds. Several severe wind gusts resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm outflow winds caused 1/4 mile visibility near the Willcox Playa due to blowing dust. The trained spotter measured a wind gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds associated with thunderstorms caused areas of blowing dust along with spotty minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Transformer fire possibly started by lightning strike.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds measured at Ocotillo and Ellsworth. Winds at Gateway airport reached 52 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Point Fire was started by lightning on Milk Point Ranch about 3 miles northeast of Pine, Arizona was managed for resource benefits. The final size of the fire was 1300 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Point Fire at around 3 PM on May 31st about 3 miles northeast of Pine, Arizona on the Mogollon Rim.  The fire was managed for resource benefits and was allowed to burn to a final size of 1300 acres by the time it was contained on June 23rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding from heavy rain covered parts of Highway 99 between 6 and 11 miles south of Leupp.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm fell over the Cottonwood area.  Rainfall rates exceeded 2.00 inches an hour in some reports. Street flooding was reported all over town by the Cottonwood Police Department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lightning caused fires near the South Rim of the Grand Canyon were allowed to burn to meet management ecological and protection objectives. The Game Reserve fire started on May 29 about nine miles southeast of the Grand Canyon Village in the Grand Canyon National Park. The Ruby started on May 25 about six miles southeast of Tusayan on the Kaibab National Forest. The fires merged and became the Ruby Complex. The Complex burned more than 4,600 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ruby Complex continued to burn with low intensity within the management area for much of the month of June. The Complex burned more than 4,600 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving cold storm system moved through Southeast Arizona late February 9th into the early morning hours of February 10th. This low pressure brought snowfall to elevations as low as 2500 feet including Tucson. Populated locations across Southeast Arizona at elevations between 3000 and 5000 feet received several inches of snowfall with well over a foot of snow across the mountain tops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell at Kitt Peak at an elevation of about 6800 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving cold storm system moved through Southeast Arizona late February 9th into the early morning hours of February 10th. This low pressure brought snowfall to elevations as low as 2500 feet including Tucson. Populated locations across Southeast Arizona at elevations between 3000 and 5000 feet received several inches of snowfall with well over a foot of snow across the mountain tops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell at elevations above 3000 feet in Eastern Pima County. Locations such as the far east and southeast side of the city of Tucson in addition to Vail and Corona De Tucson had 2 to 3 inches of snow. In the higher elevations of the Catalina Mountains there was well over a foot of snow with drifts up to 4 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving cold storm system moved through Southeast Arizona late February 9th into the early morning hours of February 10th. This low pressure brought snowfall to elevations as low as 2500 feet including Tucson. Populated locations across Southeast Arizona at elevations between 3000 and 5000 feet received several inches of snowfall with well over a foot of snow across the mountain tops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sonoita, at an elevation of just under 5000 feet had 5 inches of snow. Meanwhile, the higher elevations of the Santa Rita mountains had in excess of one foot of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the lower elevations in Cochise county including Benson and Willcox had anywhere from 2 to 4 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening pacific storm system moved into Southeast Arizona resulting in scattered showers with a couple of embedded thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A suspected illegal immigrant in the Altar valley was struck by lightning and suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog reduced the visibility to less than 1/2 mile in portions of Yuma County. After some locally heavy rainfall on Saturday, clearing skies resulted in patchy fog near Tacna and Dateland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Tacna reported dense fog with visibility less than 1/4 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system moved across the state and brought locally  heavy snow to the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of new snow reported around southern Gila county. The heaviest amount was 12 inches in the Top of the World community, about 7 miles east of Superior.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief heavy rain and small hail moved to the northwest across southern Gila County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief heavy rain and small hail about 1/4 inch diameter was reported at Roosevelt Estates. Storm total rain since Sunday morning amounted to 1.00 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system in southern California produced strong south winds across southeast Arizona. The winds remained below wind advisory criteria, averaging 20 to 30 mph and gusting to near 40 mph on the higher terrain. These winds, combined with the prevailing dry conditions, resulted in locally dense blowing dust in Pinal county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local areas of blowing dust along Interstate 10 resulted in several vehicle collisions near the Casa Grande and Eloy areas during the morning hours. A fatal collision occurred at approximately 10:30 AM near mile marker 213, between a mini-van and tractor/trailer.  Locally dense blowing dust reduced visibility, causing the mini-van to collide with the tractor/trailer from behind. This collision resulted in two fatalities and one serious injury, all of them occupants of the mini-van. The two occupants of the tractor/trailer were not injured. A total of four other accidents occurred as a result of the locally dense blowing dust, all of them near mile markers 214 and 215 on Interstate 10. One of these collisions involved six vehicles, and three of them resulted in an unknown number of injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over northwest Mexico on November 29th resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms over southeast Arizona. Despite the limited moisture available, cold air aloft associated with the storm system allowed for enough instability to generate some strong thunderstorms with small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a funnel cloud from a thunderstorm between Douglas and McNeal in Cochise County.  After obtaining pictures of this funnel cloud from the broadcast media, it was determined that this funnel was briefly in contact with the ground.  Therefore, the funnel was classified as a brief tornado touchdown.  There was no apparent damage from this EF-0 tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system triggered a few thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert.  One storm produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a foot of flowing water covered the intersection of Pierce Ferry Rd. and Charles Dr.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted near 56th street and Indian School Road. Power lines were downed by winds and left abou 15,000 customers without electricity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains resulted in flooded roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused widespread street flooding in Prescott Valley.  Water reached the front doorstep of one house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief heavy rain affected parts of the Miami and Globe area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets in a small area of Miami were closed early on Thursday morning due to the runoff from heavy rains late Wednesday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produce strong winds and large waves on Lake Powell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm over Lake Powell produced wind gusts to 54 MPH and waves up to 7 feet high.  One houseboat and 13 other boats were swamped.  Four law enforcement boats responded to 16 distress calls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pilot reported a funnel cloud over Black Canyon City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A plot reported a funnel cloud over Black Canyon City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced flooding and large hail in Skull Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four feet of water covered the road at the intersection of Copper Basin and Peavine Roads.  Water was up to the rail road tracks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture fueled an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert.  Several storms produced high winds and flash flooding.
Monsoon moisture fueled an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert.  Several storms produced high winds and flash flooding.
An unseasonably strong area of low pressure brought strong southwest winds to the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds on Lake Mead created high waves which capsized a boat in the Castle Cove area.  One of the six people on the boat was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of monsoon moisture triggered scattered thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert.  A few storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rollover accident on Interstate 40 at mile marker 58.5 killed one man (indirect fatality). Rain and wet roads were contributing factors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and monsoon moisture combined to trigger an outbreak of thunderstorms across the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain fell between 515 and 915 PM, causing water, rocks and mud to cover several roads in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prescribed burn jumped containment lines and became a wildfire southwest of Williams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twin Fire began as a prescribed burn that jumped containment lines.  The fire was moving towards the town of William where 64 homes were evacuated.  The fire threatened over 100 homes and businesses.  Smoke from the fire impacted travel on I40 by lowering the visibility. The fire reached a final size of just over 900 acres. Resources assigned to the Twin Fire included: a Type I Incident Management Team, 7 helicopters, several air tankers, 6 Hot Shot Crews, 4 Hand Crews, 33 engines, 2 dozers and 7 water tenders.  In all, over 450 personnel were assigned to the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong thunderstorms moved to the south during the afternoon. Some storms contained very heavy rain and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain,  accompanied by pea-sized hail hit the Globe area in the afternoon. Streets were briefly flooded in Globe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense blowing dust reduced the visibility to less than 1/4 mile between Eloy and Arizona City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two spotters reported dense blowing dust with visibility less than 1/4 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across the western portions of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain reported. One spotter had 0.90 inches in 1.5 hours and another reported 0.5 inches in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds associated with the passage of a sharp cold front gusted to over 30 mph and resulted in a power outage in the Yuma area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds increased during the late afternoon hours and caused a power outage to the area of San Luis and Somerton. The outage initially affectd 16,000 customers in southern Yuma County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds from the north resulted in a short power outage in Somerton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds caused power lines to contact each other and left about 1,469 APS customers without electricity on Thursday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving showers and thunderstorms dropped over an inch of rain in about an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter near Peoria measured 0.65 inches in 25 minutes. Another spotter near Buckeye also had 0.65 inches of rain, and a spotter near Laveen had 0.90 from 9 to 10:30 am.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced strong winds across the Eastern Mogollon Rim.  The Show Low ASOS recorded sustained winds around 40 MPH from late morning to mid afternoon.  The peak wind gust was 58 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought strong winds to the Little Colorado River Valley around Winslow.  The Winslow ASOS recorded sustained winds around 40 MPH with a peak wind gust of 59 MPH from around noon to mid afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm hit northern Arizona December 7th and 8th.  This storm produced heavy snow,  strong winds,  and limited visibility in the high country.  In the lower terrain,  this storm produced heavy rain,  damaging winds, and severe thunderstorms.  Freeways were closed due to snow and blowing snow,  hunters were trapped by heavy snow and falling trees,  and over 400 hundred cars slid off the roads.  The storm resulted in the largest search and rescue operations in Coconino County with nearly 50 people located from the Grand Canyon to Happy Jack.  The Department of Public Safety dispatch center in Flagstaff received 3,000 calls for service within the first 24 hours of this storm.  There were close to 550 slide offs and motorist assists, 83 non-injury collisions, and 26 injury collisions across northern Arizona. More than 14,000 customers were without power after the storm passed. Heavy rains loosened soil around boulders allowing them to fall on roadways. This storm caused school closures across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow fell in Keams Canyon.

Populated locations across Southeast Arizona at elevations between 3000 and 5000 feet received several inches of snowfall with well over a foot of snow across the mountain tops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds increased in advance of a cold front and picked up considerable dust and soil from a field adjacent to Interstate 10 in Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sudden drop in visibility due to local blowing dust contributed to a massive pileup in the eastbound lanes of Interstate 10 on Tuesday morning. Winds at the time of the accident were estimated to be about 40 mph. About 9 trucks and 13 automobiles were involved in the accidents. Some vehicles caught on fire. Three fatalities and 14 injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds knocked over a 70 tall-two foot thick-ponderosa pine tree about 20 miles east of Camp Verde.  The tree fell on a man sleeping in a tent.  The man was struck in the head and died instantly.  A friend in the tent was not injured.  Measured wind speeds include:  Prescott Love Field: 74 MPH, Crown King (Horsethief Basin RAWS) : 69 MPH,  and Mingus Mountain 70 MPH.  About 5000 homes lost powers during the storm.  At least 60 large trees fell throughout Prescott.  Thirty trees had to be cut and removed from roadways.  At least six trees fell on homes. Other trees fell on parked cars. Many homes and businesses reported roof damage or destruction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm hit northern Arizona December 7th and 8th.  This storm produced heavy snow,  strong winds,  and limited visibility in the high country.  In the lower terrain,  this storm produced heavy rain,  damaging winds, and severe thunderstorms.  Freeways were closed due to snow and blowing snow,  hunters were trapped by heavy snow and falling trees,  and over 400 hundred cars slid off the roads.  The storm resulted in the largest search and rescue operations in Coconino County with nearly 50 people located from the Grand Canyon to Happy Jack.  The Department of Public Safety dispatch center in Flagstaff received 3,000 calls for service within the first 24 hours of this storm.  There were close to 550 slide offs and motorist assists, 83 non-injury collisions, and 26 injury collisions across northern Arizona. More than 14,000 customers were without power after the storm passed. Heavy rains loosened soil around boulders allowing them to fall on roadways. This storm caused school closures across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 60 MPH were measured in the Heber-Overgaard area.  The Mogollon Airpark reported a peak wind gust of 70 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed as the cold front moved through Bellemont.  The winds were sustained up to 50 MPH with gusts to 70 MPH with zero visibility in snow and blowing snow. Over 20 inches of snow had fallen in the 20 hours before the front arrived and was piked up by the strong winds.  There were power outages in Flagstaff.  Trees and tree branches were knocked down by the winds.  I-40 was shut down from Ash Fork to Winslow.  The Flagstaff Airport had a peak wind gust of 53 MPH and Doney Park 65 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense winter storm passed through Arizona during the evening hours of December 7th and during the early morning hours of December 8th.  Heavy snowfall and very strong winds combined to produce winter storm conditions in the mountains of southeast Arizona, with blizzard conditions occurring at times.  A particularly strong pressure gradient south of this storm produced a significant nighttime high wind event across much of southeast Arizona, with many reports of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds gusted to near 70 mph across portions of eastern Pima County during the overnight hours, causing damage in several areas.  Portions of a roof blew off a residence on West Rama Drive in Green Valley, with the debris hitting several nearby mobile homes.  Winds downed several trees in the Green Valley and Sahuarita areas. |High winds also removed a roof from another residence in southwest Tucson, near the intersection of Cardinal Avenue and Valencia Road.  Strong winds also downed hundreds of trees on Mt. Lemmon, near Summerhaven.   Many of these trees damaged power lines as they fell, with dozens of homes and businesses spending several days without power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread damaging winds occurred across Cochise County during the overnight hours.  Law enforcement reported winds blew out windows of some businesses in Sierra Vista.  Wind gusts near 70 mph blew portions of a roof off a warehouse in Sierra Vista, with the debris striking nearby power lines and causing power disruptions.  Roof damage was also reported at a motel in Sierra Vista, with about half of the roof removed.  Debris from the roof landed in a nearby parking lot, damaging several cars.  High winds damaged a large sign at a restaurant, with numerous traffic signs receiving damage throughout the Sierra Vista area.  Several reports of fallen trees and broken limbs were received, and at least four people in Sierra Vista reported falling trees causing damage to their vehicles. Strong winds caused damage to numerous power poles and power lines in Sierra Vista and surrounding Cochise County, resulting in power outages for thousands of homes and businesses. Many other power poles were damaged by the strong winds near Bowie, San Simon, and Willcox. The ASOS at Fort Huachuca measured a wind gust of 71 mph at 12:38 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At strong cold front brought strong winds and blowing dust along I-40 between Flagstaff and Winslow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An anemometer along I-40 near Two Guns reported a peak wind gust go 58 MPH with frequent gusts over 50 MPH.  Blowing dust and sand from this storm caused the closure of I-40 between Meteor Crater and Winslow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Arizona during the afternoon and evening hours. Northerly winds increased to 30 to 40 MPH with higher gusts in a|few areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous power poles were reported blown down in the Phoenix area, leaving about 1200 people without power for several hours. Customers with both APS and SRP experienced power outages. The strong northerly winds, gusting to over 45 mph, also caused poor visibilities in some areas. Cross winds also resulted in air traffic delays, and even a full stoppage of arrivals at Sky Harbor airport for nearly one hour. Based on hourly weather observations from 1931 to present, the peak wind of 53 mph was the highest wind observed during the spring months of March, April and May in Phoenix. It was also the 7th highest wind gust on record in Phoenix.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN COCONINO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system approaching Arizona brought damaging winds,  blowing dust, blowing sand to northern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter near I-40 and Leupp Road reported 68 MPH winds and visibility down to 100 feet in blowing dust.  I-40 was shut down due to the strong winds and blowing dust. An anemometer on the edge of Meteor Crater recorded a peak wind gust of 91 MPH. The edge of the crater often has much stronger winds than the surrounding area due to the topography of the crater. Tuba City reported a peak wind gust of 61 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with strong to very strong winds moved across northern Arizona.  The strongest winds were observed over the White Mountains,  the Little Colorado River Valley,  the Hopi Nation and the Navajo Nation.  The winds frequently gusted into the 50 MPH range and a few gusts into the 60 MPH range were observed.  Several locations reported near zero visibility in blowing dust and sand.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Window Rock ASOS reported a peak wind gusts to 59 MPH.  Blowing dust reduced the visibility to less than one mile. A restaurant sign blew off its pedestal in Window Rock, crushed an SUV,  and injured a couple who had stopped for lunch. One person, in critical condition,  was taken to a local health service center then transported to Phoenix once the winds died down and it was safe to fly.
